currentUse: Active Roman Catholic worship site of the merged St. John Paul II Parish (one Sunday Mass at 8:30 a.m.), with St. George Independent Catholic School operating on the same campus at 2700 E. Venango St., Port Richmond
summary: St. George (Šv. Jurgio) Lithuanian church in Philadelphia's Port Richmond neighborhood is still standing and still in Catholic use. The parish, founded in 1902 as a personal parish for Lithuanian speakers, was merged effective July 1, 2019 with three other ethnic Port Richmond parishes — St. Adalbert (Polish), Mother of Divine Grace (Italian), and Nativity B.V.M. — into a single parish, renamed St. John Paul II Parish effective October 22, 2019, with St. Adalbert as the primary worship site. All four church buildings were kept open as worship sites, and as of July 2026 the parish website still lists St. George Church (2700 E. Venango St.) with a Sunday 8:30 a.m. Mass under pastor Rev. James P. Olson. The building, erected in 1920 with the school built into its first floor, retains its Lithuanian fabric: the Vytis (Lithuanian coat of arms) over the entrance, Stations of the Cross with Lithuanian inscriptions, and Lithuanian donor names beneath the stained glass. Sacramental records of the former parish are kept at St. John Paul II Parish, and the attached school continues as the independent St. George Independent Catholic School. No sale, demolition, or relegation of the building has been reported; its main exposure is its reduced status as a one-Mass secondary worship site within a four-church merged parish.

developments.headline: Archdiocese merges St. George (Lithuanian) with three other Port Richmond parishes
developments.detail: Effective July 1, 2019, St. George Parish (founded 1902), St. Adalbert (Polish, 1904), and Mother of Divine Grace (Italian, 1926) merged into Nativity B.V.M. Parish, worshiping primarily at St. Adalbert Church under pastor Fr. James Olson. The pastor assured parishioners that all four church buildings would remain open as worship sites and the three associated schools would continue for the coming year.

sources.title: Merger of 4 city parishes into one akin to Emmaus journey

developments.headline: Merged parish renamed St. John Paul II Parish
developments.detail: The consolidated Port Richmond parish (formerly Nativity B.V.M.) was renamed St. John Paul II Parish effective October 22, 2019 — the first parish in the Philadelphia Archdiocese named for John Paul II, honoring his 1976 two-week stay at St. Adalbert during the Eucharistic Congress. The article also records that a fifth parish, Our Lady Help of Christians (German), had already merged into Nativity B.V.M. in 2016.

sources.title: Merged Port Richmond parish adopts new name: St. John Paul II

developments.headline: St. John Paul II Parish active; confirmation with Archbishop Pérez
developments.detail: A CatholicPhilly photo feature on the merged parish shows it active, with more than 75 young Catholics confirmed by Archbishop Nelson Pérez on October 24, 2024, under pastor Fr. James Olson — indirect evidence the four-site merged parish remained functioning, though the feature gives no St. George-specific detail.

sources.title: Across the Aisles: St. John Paul II, Port Richmond

developments.headline: St. George Church still an active worship site with one Sunday Mass
developments.detail: The St. John Paul II Parish website currently lists St. George Church, 2700 E. Venango St., as one of its four worship sites, with a single Sunday Mass at 8:30 a.m.; pastor is Rev. James P. Olson. The other sites (St. Adalbert, Nativity B.V.M., Mother of Divine Grace) carry the fuller schedules, including Polish-language Mass at St. Adalbert; no Lithuanian-language Mass is listed anywhere in the parish.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive record's closing date is wrong: it states the parish was merged out ~2015-2016 (Closed: 2016), but the Archdiocese of Philadelphia's own parish record dates St. George (Lithuanian) 1902-2019, and CatholicPhilly documents the merger decree effective July 1, 2019. The likely source of the 2016 confusion is that a different Port Richmond parish, Our Lady Help of Christians (German), merged into Nativity B.V.M. in 2016 during the same restructuring wave; St. George survived that round and was merged three years later. The archive's 'losing its Lithuanian identity' claim is a fair characterization of the canonical outcome (no Lithuanian parish, no Lithuanian-language Mass listed), but needs refinement: the building was NOT closed — it remains an open worship site of St. John Paul II Parish with a weekly Sunday Mass, and its Lithuanian fabric (Vytis over the entrance, Lithuanian-inscribed Stations of the Cross, Lithuanian donor names under the stained glass) is still in place per Global True Lithuania. Ownership (diocese_rc) and ending type (diocese merger) are confirmed; founding year, absent in the archive, is 1902 per the Archdiocese.
gaps: No property sale, listing, or demolition record was sought beyond news/diocesan sources because the building remains in diocesan use — no real-estate transaction exists to trace. Searches for 2025-2026 news on the parish and worship site returned nothing; no announced plan to relegate or close the St. George worship site was found, but as a one-Mass secondary site in a four-church merged parish its forward exposure is real and undocumented. Global True Lithuania (undated page) claims a 2011 merger involving St. Andrew Lithuanian parish and gives the address as 3580 Salmon St. (vs. the parish site's 2700 E. Venango St. — likely the same corner property); neither claim could be corroborated in archdiocesan sources, so both are flagged rather than asserted. No Lithuanian-language Mass, diaspora reunion, or preservation campaign for this church surfaced in searches. St. George Independent Catholic School's continued operation is attested on the Archdiocese parish page (principal Dr. Joanne Walls) and the school's own site exists (stgeorgecatholic.org), but the school site returned HTTP 403, so enrollment figures and its exact governance relationship to the parish were not verified from an opened page. Identity confirmation is solid: the Lithuanian dedication is explicit in archdiocesan and news sources (no confusion with Philadelphia's Greek Orthodox or Coptic St. George congregations, which searches also surfaced).
